parsed_findings.chain_steps: Exploit injection via `dir`, then use UNION/stacked queries or error-based techniques to extract or manipulate data.
parsed_findings.impact: An attacker may read unauthorized data and potentially modify or destroy data depending on DB permissions and driver behavior.
parsed_findings.proof: With `dir=asc;--`, the query becomes `SELECT id,email FROM users ORDER BY name asc;--`.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Send a request to `list_users` with `sort=name&dir=asc;--` and observe database errors or altered results indicating injected SQL execution.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: The user-controlled `dir` parameter is interpolated into the SQL `ORDER BY` clause without validation or parameterization, enabling SQL injection.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Inject an internal target URL in `url` -> server-side `axios.get(u)` -> proxy response returned to the attacker.
parsed_findings.impact: An attacker can access internal network endpoints (including cloud instance metadata) and exfiltrate sensitive data.
parsed_findings.proof: Requesting `/fetch?url=http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/` causes the server to perform the GET and return the response body via `res.send(data.data)`.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Send an HTTP GET to `/fetch?url=http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/` and observe the server returning the fetched metadata content.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: Unvalidated user-controlled `req.query.url` is fetched server-side with `axios.get`, enabling SSRF to arbitrary internal or link-local resources.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Attacker-provided HTML/JS is stored in `inv.note` (lines 6-7) and then rendered by some client without proper escaping/encoding, triggering script execution.
parsed_findings.impact: An attacker can execute arbitrary JavaScript in users' browsers, leading to session theft, data exfiltration, or actions on behalf of victims.
parsed_findings.proof: `inv.note` is set directly from `request.json.get('note', inv.note)` (line 6) and then persisted (line 7) with no sanitization step.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: As an authorized user, send a PATCH to `/api/invoices/<inv_id>` with JSON `{"note":"<script>alert(1)</script>"}` and then view the returned note in a client that inserts it into the DOM without HTML escaping.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: The endpoint stores attacker-controlled `note` content without validation or sanitization, which can enable stored XSS when the note is later rendered unsafely.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Exploit traversal to exfiltrate sensitive local files, then use any exposed secrets/configs/keys to escalate access or discover more targets.
parsed_findings.impact: An attacker can read arbitrary files from the server filesystem (confidentiality breach) and potentially further pivot to higher-severity attacks.
parsed_findings.proof: Supplying a crafted query like file=../../../../etc/passwd causes f to retain traversal sequences (e.g., '..' segments not removed by replace) and path.join('/srv/data', f) to resolve outside /srv/data before fs.readFile reads it.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Request GET /download?file=../../../../etc/passwd and observe the server returns the contents with HTTP 200 and body text.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: The endpoint allows path traversal because it only strips a single '../' substring and then uses path.join to read an arbitrary file under /srv/data via fs.readFile.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: User controls `request.cookies['state']` -> base64 decode produces attacker bytes -> `pickle.loads` runs the pickle deserialization gadgets -> code execution occurs on the server.
parsed_findings.impact: Remote code execution in the application context, potentially leading to full system compromise, data theft, and persistent backdoors.
parsed_findings.proof: If an attacker sets the `state` cookie to a base64-encoded malicious pickle payload, `pickle.loads(base64.b64decode(raw))` will deserialize it and execute the payload.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Send a request to `/resume` with `Cookie: state=<base64(malicious_pickle)>` such that the decoded bytes trigger gadget execution during `pickle.loads`.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: The endpoint deserializes attacker-controlled cookie data with `pickle.loads`, enabling arbitrary code execution via insecure deserialization.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: 1) Authenticate as a non-support user 2) PATCH your own account setting `isSupport` (and any other privileged attributes) 3) Use the now-elevated account to access support-only update capabilities.
parsed_findings.impact: An attacker can elevate their role (e.g., `isSupport`) and potentially modify other users’ data by bypassing intended access controls.
parsed_findings.proof: Because it uses `Object.assign(user, req.body)` (line 6) and then persists the entire mutated user with `db.users.save(user)` (line 7), any field included in `req.body` can be written to the database.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Authenticate as a normal user, send `PATCH /api/users/<your_id>` with body `{"isSupport":true}`, and then verify the account has support privileges and can patch other users.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: The route performs mass assignment by merging attacker-controlled `req.body` into the user object without an allowlist, enabling privilege escalation via sensitive fields.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Attacker submits malicious markdown -> `markdown.markdown()` produces unsafe HTML -> server stores it in `html` -> `/comments` returns it via `db.all_comments()` where client-side rendering can execute it.
parsed_findings.impact: An attacker can execute arbitrary JavaScript in other users’ browsers, leading to session theft, UI redressing, and account compromise.
parsed_findings.proof: Send `body` as `` so `rendered` contains attacker-controlled executable markup that gets persisted in `db.insert_comment({'html': rendered,...})`.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: POST `/comments` with JSON like `{ "body": "" }` and then GET `/comments` to observe execution when the client renders the returned `html`.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Negative `amount` bypasses the `src < amt` guard and causes both `set_balance` updates to apply in the attacker’s favor.
parsed_findings.impact: An attacker can create or transfer value by manipulating balances with negative amounts.
parsed_findings.proof: Because it computes `src-amt` and `to_balance+amt` after only checking `if src < amt`, `amt=-N` makes `src-amt=src+N` (crediting the sender) while decrementing the recipient.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Send a request like `POST /transfer` with JSON `{"to":2,"amount":-100}` while your account balance is at least 0.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: The endpoint does not validate that `amount` is positive, so a negative `amount` reverses the arithmetic and can increase the sender's balance.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Non-atomic `get_balance` + separate `set_balance` calls allow interleaving so the guard reflects stale state across requests.
parsed_findings.impact: Concurrent transfers can overdraw the sender or result in incorrect balance states, potentially creating/duplicating value.
parsed_findings.proof: With concurrent requests, both can read the same `src`, both pass `if src < amt`, and both then execute `set_balance(current_user.id, src-amt)` and credit the recipient, leading to overdraft/inconsistency.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: If `current_user` balance is 100, send two concurrent `POST /transfer` requests each with JSON `{"to":2,"amount":80}`.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: The balance check and updates are not performed atomically, enabling race conditions where concurrent transfers can bypass the insufficient-funds check.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Use the returned decoded claims (e.g., roles/user id) to access protected endpoints that rely on `verifyToken` output.
parsed_findings.impact: Authentication/authorization can be bypassed, enabling privilege escalation based on forged JWT claims.
parsed_findings.proof: Because `jwt.decode` is used only when `jwt.verify` throws and no cryptographic validation is performed in that branch, forged/invalid-signature JWTs can still be accepted in `NODE_ENV === 'production'`.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Set `process.env.NODE_ENV = 'production'`, send an `Authorization: Bearer <forged JWT with invalid signature>` header, and observe `verifyToken` returning the decoded payload instead of throwing.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: In production, the code falls back to `jwt.decode(t)` after `jwt.verify` fails, which can return attacker-controlled token claims without verifying the signature.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Authenticated user crafts `id=0` to reach the SQL query path and receives the fetched row (or `{}` if none).
parsed_findings.impact: If a user row with `id=0` exists, the endpoint can disclose that user's `email` to any authenticated user.
parsed_findings.proof: The code returns `403` when `uid` is truthy (`if uid: return ...`) but only executes the database query when `uid` is falsy, which occurs for `id=0`.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Log in to obtain a valid session and request `GET /u?id=0`.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: The authorization logic is inverted so authenticated users can bypass the forbidden check by passing `id=0`, potentially retrieving a user record they should not access.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Once a valid signature is recovered, send any JSON payload to trigger the protected `process_event` logic.
parsed_findings.impact: An attacker can forge `X-Sig`, reach `process_event(json.loads(body))`, and execute unintended event processing as an authenticated caller.
parsed_findings.proof: Python string equality may short-circuit on the first differing character, so response time can correlate with how many leading hex characters of `got` match `want`.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Send many requests to `/hook` with attacker-controlled `X-Sig` values and identical body, measure response times for failures, then iteratively improve `X-Sig` by keeping the prefix that yields the smallest/consistent timing until the full HMAC hex string is matched.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: The signature check uses non-constant-time equality (`got == want`), enabling an observable timing side-channel to gradually recover a valid signature.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Combine with a valid-signature capability to reach the JSON parse path and repeatedly force 500 responses.
parsed_findings.impact: Attackers who can get a request through signature validation (e.g., via a recovered/compromised signature) can crash the handler repeatedly and disrupt the webhook service.
parsed_findings.proof: There is no `try/except` around `json.loads(body)`, so invalid JSON will raise and bubble up to Flask’s default error handler.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Provide a request where `got == want` is true (e.g., using a valid signature) and set the body bytes to an invalid JSON string, then observe an internal server error instead of a controlled failure.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: The code calls `json.loads(body)` without error handling, so malformed JSON can trigger an uncaught exception and return a 500 error (DoS) when signature verification passes.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: 1) Host a malicious page behind `attacker.com` 2) Send victims a link to `https://victim-site/go?next=//attacker.com` 3) Browser follows the open redirect to attacker-controlled content.
parsed_findings.impact: An attacker can craft links to this site that transparently redirect users to external phishing or malicious destinations.
parsed_findings.proof: Requesting `/go?next=//attacker.com` results in a redirect `Location: //attacker.com` (protocol-relative) to an attacker-controlled domain.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Send `GET /go?next=//attacker.com` and observe the `Location` header / browser navigation to `https://attacker.com`.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: The endpoint validates `next` only by requiring it starts with `/`, which allows protocol-relative URLs like `//attacker.com` to bypass and trigger an external open redirect.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: `resolve_entities=True` plus `no_network=False` permits external entity expansion during `fromstring`, and `doc.findtext('title')` returns the expanded entity content to the caller.
parsed_findings.impact: An attacker can read sensitive local files and/or trigger outbound requests (SSRF) to attacker-controlled or internal network endpoints via external entity resolution.
parsed_findings.proof: Because `etree.XMLParser(resolve_entities=True, no_network=False)` is used on `etree.fromstring(request.data, parser=parser)`, a POST body with a malicious `DOCTYPE` can cause the `<title>` content returned by `findtext` to include resolved external entity data.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Send a POST to `/xml/import` with a crafted XML body that defines an external entity in a `DOCTYPE` and references it inside `<title>` (e.g., `<!DOCTYPE x [<!ENTITY xxe SYSTEM "file:///etc/passwd">]><root><title>&xxe;</title></root>`).
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: The code enables XML external entity resolution and parses attacker-controlled XML with entity expansion, allowing XXE-driven data exfiltration.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: (1) Submit arbitrary `email`/`password` to `/login`; (2) `db.users.findOne(q)` runs but the next conditional is always true; (3) the handler follows the login-success path unconditionally (response logic not shown in snippet).
parsed_findings.impact: An attacker can obtain unauthorized access without valid credentials.
parsed_findings.proof: The code contains `if ( true )` before ending the handler, so the success branch would execute regardless of the provided credentials.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Send any POST `/login` request with arbitrary `email` and `password` values and observe the endpoint behaving as if authentication succeeded.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: Authentication is effectively bypassed because the login success condition is hardcoded to always evaluate true.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: (1) Craft `req.body.email`/`req.body.password` to include query operators; (2) the operator objects become part of `q`; (3) `findOne(q)` evaluates the injected operators and may return an unintended document; (4) login proceeds according to the (also problematic) conditional logic.
parsed_findings.impact: An attacker may manipulate the query to bypass credential checks or enumerate/impersonate users depending on how `findOne` evaluates operator objects.
parsed_findings.proof: The query `q` is created as `{ email: req.body.email, password: req.body.password }` and passed directly to `db.users.findOne(q)`.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: If `db.users.findOne` is Mongo-like, submit `email` (or `password`) as an object containing query operators (e.g., `{"$ne":"x"}`) and check whether authentication succeeds without matching a real user record.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: The query object is built directly from user-controlled `req.body` without sanitization, enabling NoSQL-style injection if the backing `findOne` interpreter treats operator objects specially.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Leverage SSRF to reach internal-only HTTP services, then use the returned `size` (and differences in behavior) to enumerate accessible resources.
parsed_findings.impact: An attacker can probe internal services and potentially access otherwise-restricted endpoints via the server’s network.
parsed_findings.proof: Supplying a URL like `http://127.0.0.1:port/` (or internal metadata endpoints) will cause the server to perform `requests.get(url)` and return the response size.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Send a POST to `/avatar/fetch` with JSON body `{"url":"http://127.0.0.1:8080/"}` and observe the server makes the internal request and returns `{size: ...}`.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: User-controlled `url` is fetched server-side without allowlisting or network restrictions, enabling SSRF to internal network resources.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Use SSRF-capable URL selection (or external large responses) to trigger unbounded in-memory downloads until the application or host becomes unstable.
parsed_findings.impact: An attacker can exhaust server memory or degrade service by forcing large downloads from the server.
parsed_findings.proof: Point `url` to a large file response (or endpoint that returns an arbitrarily large body) and the server will read it fully before computing `len(data)`.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: POST to `/avatar/fetch` with `{"url":"https://example.com/very_large_file"}` (or attacker-controlled endpoint) and monitor memory/latency while it downloads.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: The server downloads the entire remote response into memory (`requests.get(...).content`) without size limits, enabling memory/DoS via large payloads.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Send an oversized payload to `gets` to corrupt the stack until control-flow is influenced.
parsed_findings.impact: An attacker can cause denial of service or potentially achieve code execution by overwriting control data on the stack.
parsed_findings.proof: Supplying input longer than 63 bytes overwrites adjacent stack data (including saved frame/return address) before `puts('ok')` executes.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Compile and run, then enter a string longer than 64 bytes (e.g., 200 'A's) to observe a crash or control-flow corruption.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: The use of `gets(buf)` writes attacker-controlled data into a fixed-size stack buffer without bounds checking, enabling stack buffer overflow.

parsed_findings.impact: An attacker able to read the app’s local storage (e.g., rooted device, malware, or backup access) can steal the token and impersonate the user/session.
parsed_findings.proof: The code writes the token via `p.edit().putString('access_token', token).apply()` to `getSharedPreferences('auth', Context.MODE_PRIVATE)` with no cryptographic protection.

parsed_findings.vuln_claim: saveToken stores the access_token in Android SharedPreferences without encryption, leaving a cleartext session token on disk.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Read or obtain the source, recover `KEY`, decrypt collected `iv+ciphertext` blobs to view plaintext.
parsed_findings.impact: Confidentiality failure: encrypted data can be recovered offline and future compromises persist as long as the key remains unchanged.
parsed_findings.proof: `KEY = b'0123456789abcdef'` is a constant shared across all encryption calls.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Extract the value of `KEY` from the code, then decrypt any `enc()` output by splitting `iv = ct[:16]` and decrypting the remaining ciphertext with AES-CBC using the same static key and IV.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: The AES encryption key is hardcoded in source code, making all ciphertext decryptable by anyone who can read the code.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Intercept ciphertext, modify `iv`/previous block to induce controlled plaintext changes, forward the altered ciphertext to a decrypting system and observe the altered output/behavior.
parsed_findings.impact: Integrity failure: an attacker who can tamper with ciphertext can modify the decrypted plaintext (and potentially bypass application logic) without detection.
parsed_findings.proof: The function returns only `iv + cipher.encrypt(pad(data, 16))` and does not append a MAC/tag or use an AEAD mode.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Take a valid ciphertext, flip chosen bits in the IV or a ciphertext block so that the corresponding bits in the next decrypted plaintext block are predictably altered when CBC is decrypted.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: AES-CBC is used without any integrity/authentication mechanism, enabling ciphertext malleability attacks.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Trigger the bug by supplying `out==NULL` (or `in==NULL`) with `out_sz>0` to bypass the faulty guard and reach the dereferences on lines 4-6.
parsed_findings.impact: An attacker-controlled NULL argument can cause a denial-of-service crash and potentially undefined behavior depending on build/runtime checks.
parsed_findings.proof: With `out==NULL` and `out_sz>0`, the guard on line 3 does not return (because `out || out_sz == 0` evaluates false) and execution reaches `memcpy(out, in, n)` on line 5 and `out[n]='\\0'` on line 6, dereferencing NULL.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Call `copy_name(
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: The function incorrectly checks output pointer validity (`if (out || out_sz == 0) return -1;`), so passing `out==NULL` (or `in==NULL`) leads to NULL pointer dereference via `memcpy(out,...)` and `out[n]='\\0'` (or `strnlen(in,...)`).
